data.title: Kura rises after early-stage trial data for kidney cancer therapy
data.description:
Kura Oncology (KURA) added ~5% in the premarket on Friday after releasing positive data from a Phase 1 trial for its next-gen farnesyl transferase inhibitor, darlifarnib (KO-2806), in patients with clear cell renal cell carcinoma, a type of kidney cancer.

Citing a subset analysis of the ongoing FIT-001 clinical trial, the cancer drug developer stated that darlifarnib led to tumor shrinkage in 75% of patients when combined with cabozantinib, a tyrosine kinase inhibitor marketed by Exelixis (EXEL) as Cabometyx.

The analysis focused on 16 patients who had previously received cabozantinib, a group the company said is unlikely to respond to another round of cabozantinib therapy.

Additional results from FIT-001 indicate a 44% objective response rate and a 94% disease control with treatment durations ranging from 8 to 86 weeks. At the time of the data cut, six patients remained on therapy, according to the results presented at a kidney cancer event in France.

“These data highlight the potential of darlifarnib to overcome resistance to prior cabozantinib and enhance the activity of VEGF TKIs in patients with advanced RCC,” Kura’s (KURA) medical chief Mollie Leoni remarked.

data.title: Autoliv Non-GAAP EPS of $2.05 beats by $0.21, revenue of $2.75B beats by $140M
data.description:
• Autoliv press release (ALV): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $2.05 beats by $0.21.
  • Revenue of $2.75B (+6.6% Y/Y) beats by $140M.
  • Shares +10% PM.

data.title: Autoliv rallies after seeing strong results in Asia in Q1
data.description:
Autoliv (ALV) reported total sales were up 6.8% and organic sales were up 0.8% in Q1 in what it said was a "better-than-anticipated" performance. The company noted that regional and customer LVP mix is estimated to have impacted sales positively by about 1.5 percentage points, while tariff compensations added around 0.5 percentage points. Organic sales outperformed LVP significantly in China and Asia as a whole, while underperforming in the Americas.

Adjusted operating income decreased by 3.9% during the quarter. Free cash flow to -$159M for the period. Earnings of $2.05 per share were better than the consensus estimate by $0.22.

"Our positive trend in Asia continued, with strong growth in India, South Korea, and China. In China, we continued to grow faster than LVP, especially with the Chinese OEMs, outperforming by 40pp. In India, we grew sales organically by 38%, reflecting mainly the trend of increased safety content in vehicles in India, as well as the continued high level of LVP growth. We continue to expand our production capabilities in India, investing in additional inflator production capacity for future growth," highlighted CEO Mikael Bratt.

Looking ahead, Autoliv (ALV) said it is difficult to fully assess the likely impacts of the ongoing geopolitical challenges, as the situation remains fluid. Autoliv (ALV) is monitoring developments while preparing for various scenarios, including different mitigation strategies. 

Shares of Autoliv (ALV) rose 9.8% in premarket action.

data.title: JPMorgan turns bearish on Clorox due to earnings pressures
data.description:
JPMorgan lowered its rating on The Clorox Company (CLX) to Underweight from Neutral.

Analyst Andrea Teixeria and her team think Clorox (CLX) management has been executing the digital transformation well and accelerating innovation but warned of below-trend category growth lingering due to exogenous factors. Clorox's (CLX) high exposure to categories where private labels have a strong presence, especially trash bags and surface cleaning, was also seen as a negative. Cost pressures from diesel and resins are also expected to add to the downside risk for Clorox's (CLX) guidance.

"Should the conflict last long, subjecting CLX (and all companies) to higher prices for oil-based raw materials, mitigating efforts include productivity savings and pulling back on promotions," noted Teixeria.

Clorox (CLX) is lined up to report third-quarter earnings on April 30.

Shares of Clorox (CLX) were down 0.8% in premarket trading on Friday.

data.title: Dire straits for aviation as the industry hits turbulence
data.description:
Now look at them yo-yos... Hard times are coming for the aviation industry, and it's not only because of the TSA shutdown. Airlines have hiked ticket fares and baggage fees in response to the Iran war and soaring prices for jet fuel, which makes up around 30% of airlines' overall expenses, according to the International Air Transport Association. Elsewhere, IEA Director Fatih Birol warned that Europe has "maybe six weeks" left of remaining jet fuel supplies, given the energy crisis stemming from the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

That ain't workin': "In the past, there was a group called 'Dire Straits.' It's a dire strait now, and it is going to have major implications for the global economy," Birol continued. "The longer it goes, the worse it will be for the economic growth and inflation around the world." Congressman calls on airline CEOs to lower prices when fuel costs drop

The situation is already having profound effects across the sector. Budget carrier Spirit Airlines (FLYYQ), which operates on the thinnest of margins, might soon be liquidated following its second bankruptcy in two years. Capacity is also starting to be scaled back, with United Airlines (UAL), KLM (AFLYY), Qantas (QABSY), and others reducing their flight schedules. Lufthansa's (DLAKY) CityLine is even closing up shop, grounding all 27 of its aircraft as of tomorrow (it was originally set to cease operations by 2028).

We got to move these... deals: With organic growth stalling for the immediate future and concerns over profitability, companies have been entertaining M&A to ensure their competitiveness. Reports this week suggested that United (UAL) had discussed an American Airlines (AAL) merger with senior government officials, which would combine two of the largest U.S. carriers. Earlier this month, Transportation Department Secretary Sean Duffy also said he sees "room" for more M&A activity in the airline industry, noting President Trump "loves to see big deals happen." Airline outlook remains uncertain - Bernstein

data.title: Anthropic CEO to meet Susie Wiles at White House Friday: report
data.description:
Anthropic (ANTHRO) CEO Dario Amodei is set to meet White House Chief of Staff Susie Wiles on Friday, Axios reported, in what could mark a breakthrough in the company’s dispute with the Pentagon over AI use.

The tensions date back to March, when Anthropic filed civil suits against the U.S. Department of Defense after it terminated a contract with the company and labeled it a “supply-chain risk,” citing ideological differences over AI deployment.

Despite the conflict, Anthropic may see renewed support from the White House. The Trump Administration is exploring giving federal agencies access to its latest Claude model, Mythos, according to a Bloomberg report.

The model is viewed as highly capable, particularly in cybersecurity applications, though its advanced capabilities have also raised concerns about potential misuse.

Anthropic co-founder Jack Clark said earlier this week that the company, which some investors have valued at $800B, was in discussions with the Trump administration about Mythos.

A source familiar with the discussions told Axios that “It would be grossly irresponsible for the U.S. government to deprive itself of the technological leaps that the new model presents. It would be a gift to China."

Some parts of the U.S. intelligence community, plus the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA, part of Homeland Security), are testing Mythos. Treasury and others want it, the Axios report noted.

Anthropic (ANTHRO) did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

So far, Mythos, which was originally leaked last month, has been made available to some of the biggest companies in tech. It was made available in preview to roughly 50 companies and organizations that deal with critical internet infrastructure, including Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), and the Linux Foundation, according to previous reports.

Amazon and Google are among the investors in Anthropic.

data.title: Air New Zealand set to offer world's first sleep pods in the sky
data.description:
Air New Zealand (ANZFF) will soon offer the world's first sleep pods for its economy passengers, with bookings starting next month.

The pods, called Economy Skynest, will include six lie-flat nests arranged like bunk beds. Each nest is 6.6 feet in length and width at the shoulder is around 25 inches, tapering to about 16 inches at the feet.

Economy or premium economy passengers will have the option of adding a four-hour session in the pod, with two sessions offered on each flight initially. Each session will cost an additional NZ$495 ($291.46).

The Skynest pods will include a full-length mattress, bedding, ambient lighting, a privacy curtain, ventilation and charging ports.

"You'll need to get in and out of the nest on your own, which may involve bending, kneeling, crawling, or climbing," according to  Air New Zealand's (ANZFF) website. "Top and middle nests are elevated, while bottom nests are at floor level."

The pods will be fitted between economy and premium cabins on the airline's Boeing (BA) 787-9 planes. Bookings will open May 18 for ultra long-haul flights between New York and Auckland scheduled for November.

data.title: OpenAI launches GPT-Rosalind, an AI model for life sciences research
data.description:
Shares of leading life sciences companies will be in focus on Friday after OpenAI launched GPT-Rosalind, an AI model designed to accelerate drug discovery and support biology-related research work.

In a statement on Thursday, the AI start-up backed by Microsoft (MSFT) said that GPT-Rosalind is specifically designed to expedite the early stages of discovery by helping with tasks such as evidence synthesis, hypothesis generation, and experimental planning.

The model will help accelerate a process that currently takes about 10–15 years from target discovery to drug approval in the U.S., the company said, adding that it is the first release in its GPT‑Rosalind life sciences model series.

The model, named after Rosalind Franklin, a British scientist credited with her early contributions that led to the discovery of DNA structure, is now available as a research preview in ChatGPT, Codex, and the API for qualified ​customers, the company said.

The Sam Altman-led firm is also launching a free life sciences research plugin for ‌Codex, which it said will help scientists connect models to more than ​50 scientific ​tools and data sources.

The company added that it is collaborating with clients, including Amgen (AMGN), Moderna (MRNA), and Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO), to apply GPT‑Rosalind across their workflows.

Stocks to watch include major contract research organizations such as IQVIA Holdings (IQV), ICON Public Limited (ICLR), Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO), Charles River Laboratories International (CRL), Medpace Holdings (MEDP), Inotiv (NOTV), and Fortrea Holdings (FTRE).
